title: Proof where no attesting witness found.
content: If no such attesting witness can be found, it must be proved that the attestation of one attesting witness at least is in his handwriting, and that the signature of the person executing the document is in the handwriting of that person. 32
summary: If a document has witnesses who are supposed to sign it, but one or more of them cannot be found, the court will look for other ways to prove that the document is genuine. To do this, the court will check if the signature of the witness who signed the document matches their handwriting. If it does, and the signature of the person who created the document also matches their handwriting, then the court will consider the document as genuine.

subsections.text: If no such attesting witness can be found, it must be proved that the attestation of one attesting witness at least is in his handwriting, and that the signature of the person executing the document is in the handwriting of that person. 32

title: Admission of execution by party to attested document.
content: The admission of a party to an attested document of its execution by himself shall be sufficient proof of its execution as against him, though it be a document required by law to be attested.
summary: When a person signs a document that requires witnesses to verify its authenticity, and they admit in court that they signed it, this admission is enough to prove the document's execution, even if the document is one that needs to be attested by law. This means that if a person admits to signing a document, the court will accept this as proof of the document's authenticity, without needing further evidence.

subsections.text: The admission of a party to an attested document of its execution by himself shall be sufficient proof of its execution as against him, though it be a document required by law to be attested.

title: Proof when attesting witness denies execution.
content: If the attesting witness denies or does not recollect the execution of the document, its execution may be proved by other evidence.
summary: If someone signs a document as a witness, but later denies or claims they don't remember signing it, the court can still consider other evidence to prove that the document was indeed signed. This other evidence can help establish the truth about the document's execution.

subsections.text: If the attesting witness denies or does not recollect the execution of the document, its execution may be proved by other evidence.

title: Proof of document not required by law to be attested.
content: An attested document not required by law to be attested may be proved as if it was unattested.
summary: If a document doesn't need to be attested by law, you can still use it as evidence in court even if it's not attested. To prove its authenticity, you can treat it as if it was never attested in the first place, and you'll need to provide other evidence to show that it's genuine.

subsections.text: An attested document not required by law to be attested may be proved as if it was unattested.

title: Comparison of signature, writing or seal with others admitted or proved.
content: (1) In order to ascertain whether a signature, writing or seal is that of the person by whom it purports to have been written or made, any signature, writing, or seal admitted or proved to the satisfaction of the Court to have been written or made by that person may be compared with the one which is to be proved, although that signature, writing or seal has not been produced or proved for any other purpose. (2) The Court may direct any person present in Court to write any words or figures for the purpose of enabling the Court to compare the words or figures so written with any words or figures alleged to have been written by such person. (3) This section applies also, with any necessary modifications, to finger impressions.
summary:
To prove if a signature, writing, or seal belongs to a person, the court can compare it with another signature, writing, or seal that has been proven to be from the same person. This can be done even if the second signature, writing, or seal is not being used for any other purpose in the case. 

The court can also ask someone present in the court to write some words or figures so that the court can compare them with the ones in question. This helps the court determine if the writing style or signature is the same. This rule also applies to finger impressions, with some necessary changes.

content: In order to ascertain whether a digital signature is that of the person by whom it purports to have been affixed, the Court may direct (a) that person or the Controller or the Certifying Authority to produce the Digital Signature Certificate; (b) any other person to apply the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate and verify the digital signature purported to have been affixed by that person. title: Public and private documents.
content: (1) The following documents are public documents: (a) documents forming the acts, or records of the acts (i) of the sovereign authority; (ii) of official bodies and tribunals; and (iii) of public officers, legislative, judicial and executive of India or of a foreign country; (b) public records kept in any State or Union territory of private documents. (2) All other documents except the documents referred to in sub-section (1) are private.
summary:
In India, documents can be classified into two main categories: public and private documents. 

Public documents are official records or acts of the government, its authorities, or public officers. This includes documents related to laws, court decisions, and official actions taken by the government or its representatives. These documents are considered trustworthy and reliable because they come from a trusted source.

Private documents, on the other hand, are documents that do not belong to the government or its authorities. These can be personal documents, business records, or any other type of document that is not officially sanctioned by the government.

(2) All other documents except the documents referred to in sub-section (1) are private.

title: Certified copies of public documents.
content: Every public officer having the custody of a public document, which any person has a right to inspect, shall give that person on demand a copy of it on payment of the legal fees therefor, together with a certificate written at the foot of such copy that it is a true copy of such document or part thereof, as the case may be, and such certificate shall be dated and subscribed by such officer with his name and his official title, and shall be sealed, whenever such officer is authorised by law to make use of a seal; and such copies so certified shall be called certified copies. Explanation.Any officer who, by the ordinary course of official duty, is authorised to deliver such copies, shall be deemed to have the custody of such documents within the meaning of this section.
summary: If you need a copy of a public document, such as a birth or death certificate, a government officer in charge of it must provide you with a true copy on payment of the required fees. This copy will have a certificate at the bottom, signed and dated by the officer, stating that it is a genuine copy of the original document. This certified copy is considered valid evidence in court and can be used to prove the authenticity of the original document.

Instead, you can bring a certified copy of the document, which is a copy that has been verified and authenticated by the relevant authorities. This certified copy can be used as evidence in court to prove the contents of the original document.

title: Proof of other official documents.
content: The following public documents may be proved as follows: 33 (a) Acts, orders or notifications of the Central Government in any of its Ministries and Departments or of any State Government or any Department of any State Government or Union territory Administration (i) by the records of the Departments, certified by the head of those Departments respectively; or (ii) by any document purporting to be printed by order of any such Government; (b) the proceedings of Parliament or a State Legislature, by the journals of those bodies respectively, or by published Acts or abstracts, or by copies purporting to be printed by order of the Government concerned; (c) proclamations, orders or Regulations issued by the President of India or the Governor of a State or the Administrator or Lieutenant Governor of a Union territory, by copies or extracts contained in the Official Gazette; (d) the Acts of the Executive or the proceedings of the Legislature of a foreign country, by journals published by their authority, or commonly received in that country as such, or by a copy certified under the seal of the country or sovereign, or by a recognition thereof in any Central Act; (e) the proceedings of a municipal or local body in a State, by a copy of such proceedings, certified by the legal keeper thereof, or by a printed book purporting to be published by the authority of such body; (f) public documents of any other class in a foreign country, by the original or by a copy certified by the legal keeper thereof, with a certificate under the seal of a Notary Public, or of an Indian Consul or diplomatic agent, that the copy is duly certified by the officer having the legal custody of the original, and upon proof of the character of the document according to the law of the foreign country. Presumptions as to documents
summary:
This section explains how certain official documents can be used as evidence in court. 

To prove these documents, you can use the following methods:

- For documents from the Central or State Government, you can use records from the relevant department, certified by the head of that department, or a printed document that appears to be ordered by the government.

- For documents related to Parliament or State Legislature, you can use their journals, published Acts, or abstracts, or copies that appear to be printed by order of the government.

- For proclamations, orders, or regulations issued by the President, Governor, or Administrator, you can use copies or extracts from the Official Gazette.

- For Acts or proceedings of foreign countries, you can use journals published by their authority, or copies certified under the seal of the country or sovereign, or recognition in a Central Act.

- For documents from municipal or local bodies, you can use a copy certified by the legal keeper, or a printed book that appears to be published by the authority of the body.

- For public documents from foreign countries, you can use the original or a certified copy, along with a certificate from a Notary Public, Indian Consul, or diplomatic agent, proving the document's authenticity and character according to the foreign country's law.

These documents are considered reliable evidence in court because they are official and come from trusted sources.

title: Presumption as to genuineness of certified copies.
content: (1) The Court shall presume to be genuine every document purporting to be a certificate, certified copy or other document, which is by law declared to be admissible as evidence of any particular fact and which purports to be duly certified by any officer of the Central Government or of a State Government: Provided that such document is substantially in the form and purports to be executed in the manner directed by law in that behalf. (2) The Court shall also presume that any officer by whom any such document purports to be signed or certified, held, when he signed it, the official character which he claims in such paper.
summary:
When a court is presented with a document that is certified by a government officer, such as a copy of a birth certificate or a police report, it will assume that the document is genuine and authentic, provided it looks like it was created in the right way and has the right official stamps or signatures. 

This means that if the document says it's a copy of something and it looks like it should be, the court will take it at face value and not question its authenticity. Additionally, the court will also assume that the officer who signed the document was who they claimed to be at the time they signed it.

title: Presumption as to documents produced as record of evidence, etc.
content: Whenever any document is produced before any Court, purporting to be a record or memorandum of the evidence, or of any part of the evidence, given by a witness in a judicial proceeding or before any officer authorised by law to take such evidence or to be a statement or confession by any prisoner or accused person, taken in accordance with law, and purporting to be signed by any Judge or Magistrate, or by any such officer as aforesaid, the Court shall presume that (i) the document is genuine; (ii) any statements as to the circumstances under which it was taken, purporting to be made by the person signing it, are true; and (iii) such evidence, statement or confession was duly taken.
summary: When a document is presented in court, claiming to be a record of a witness's testimony, a statement, or a confession, the court will assume it's real and genuine. The court will also trust the statements made by the person who signed the document about the circumstances under which it was taken. Additionally, the court will assume that the evidence, statement, or confession was properly collected according to the law. This presumption is made unless the other party can prove otherwise.

title: Presumption as to Gazettes, newspapers, and other documents.
content: The Court shall presume the genuineness of every document purporting to be the Official Gazette, or to be a newspaper or journal, and 34 of every document purporting to be a document directed by any law to be kept by any person, if such document is kept substantially in the form required by law and is produced from proper custody. Explanation.For the purposes of this section and section 92, document is said to be in proper custody if it is in the place in which, and looked after by the person with whom such document is required to be kept; but no custody is improper if it is proved to have had a legitimate origin, or if the circumstances of the particular case are such as to render that origin probable.
summary: If a document is an official government publication, a newspaper, or a document that must be kept by law, the court will assume it's genuine if it's in the right form and comes from a safe place. A document is considered in a safe place if it's kept where it's supposed to be and is looked after by the person responsible for it. However, if the document's origin can be explained or seems likely, then its custody is not considered improper.

title: Presumption as to Gazettes in electronic or digital record.
content: The Court shall presume the genuineness of every electronic or digital record purporting to be the Official Gazette, or purporting to be electronic or digital record directed by any law to be kept by any person, if such electronic or digital record is kept substantially in the form required by law and is produced from proper custody. Explanation.For the purposes of this section and section 93 electronic records are said to be in proper custody if they are in the place in which, and looked after by the person with whom such document is required to be kept; but no custody is improper if it is proved to have had a legitimate origin, or the circumstances of the particular case are such as to render that origin probable.
summary:
If a court is presented with an electronic or digital record that claims to be an official government document or a document required by law to be kept by someone, the court will assume it's genuine if it's kept in a way that's similar to what the law requires and is produced from a trusted source. 

To be considered in proper custody, the document must be stored in the place and with the person who is supposed to keep it. However, if the origin of the document is legitimate or its origin is likely, then it's still considered to be in proper custody, even if it's not stored exactly as required by law.

subsections.text: The Court shall presume the genuineness of every electronic or digital record purporting to be the Official Gazette, or purporting to be electronic or digital record directed by any law to be kept by any person, if such electronic or digital record is kept substantially in the form required by law and is produced from proper custody.

For the purposes of this section and section 93 electronic records are said to be in proper custody if they are in the place in which, and looked after by the person with whom such document is required to be kept; but no custody is improper if it is proved to have had a legitimate origin, or the circumstances of the particular case are such as to render that origin probable.

title: Presumption as to maps or plans made by authority of Government.
content: The Court shall presume that maps or plans purporting to be made by the authority of the Central Government or any State Government were so made, and are accurate; but maps or plans made for the purposes of any cause must be proved to be accurate.
summary: When a map or plan is made by the Indian government or a state government, the court will assume it's genuine and accurate unless someone proves otherwise. However, if the map or plan is made specifically for a court case, its accuracy needs to be proven by the person presenting it as evidence.

subsections.text: The Court shall presume that maps or plans purporting to be made by the authority of the Central Government or any State Government were so made, and are accurate; but maps or plans made for the purposes of any cause must be proved to be accurate.

title: Presumption as to collections of laws and reports of decisions.
content: The Court shall presume the genuineness of, every book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of any country, and to contain any of the laws of that country, and of every book purporting to contain reports of decisions of the Courts of such country.
summary: When a book is presented in court claiming to be a collection of laws or decisions from a government or court of a country, the court will assume it is genuine unless proven otherwise. This means the court will initially accept the book as authentic, but the opposing party can still challenge its authenticity if they have evidence to the contrary.

subsections.text: The Court shall presume the genuineness of, every book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of any country, and to contain any of the laws of that country, and of every book purporting to contain reports of decisions of the Courts of such country.

title: Presumption as to powers-of-attorney.
content: The Court shall presume that every document purporting to be a power-of-attorney, and to have been executed before, and authenticated by, a Notary Public, or any Court, Judge, Magistrate, Indian Consul or Vice-Consul, or representative of the Central Government, was so executed and authenticated.
summary: When a document claims to be a power-of-attorney, the court will assume it is genuine and was properly signed and verified by a Notary Public, a judge, a magistrate, an Indian Consul or Vice-Consul, or a representative of the Central Government. This means the court will accept the document as authentic unless someone proves otherwise.

subsections.text: The Court shall presume that every document purporting to be a power-of-attorney, and to have been executed before, and authenticated by, a Notary Public, or any Court, Judge, Magistrate, Indian Consul or Vice-Consul, or representative of the Central Government, was so executed and authenticated.

title: Presumption as to electronic agreements.
content: The Court shall presume that every electronic record purporting to be an agreement containing the electronic or digital signature of the parties was so concluded by affixing the electronic or digital signature of the parties.
summary: When a court is presented with an electronic agreement, like a contract signed digitally, it will assume that the agreement is genuine and was signed by the parties involved, as long as their digital or electronic signatures are present. This means that the court will accept the agreement as authentic without needing further proof, unless there's a strong reason to doubt its validity.

subsections.text: The Court shall presume that every electronic record purporting to be an agreement containing the electronic or digital signature of the parties was so concluded by affixing the electronic or digital signature of the parties.

title: Presumption as to electronic records and electronic signatures.
content: (1) In any proceeding involving a secure electronic record, the Court shall presume unless contrary is proved, that the secure electronic record has not been altered since the specific point of time to which the secure status relates. (2) In any proceeding, involving secure electronic signature, the Court shall presume unless the contrary is proved that (a) the secure electronic signature is affixed by subscriber with the intention of signing or approving the electronic record; (b) except in the case of a secure electronic record or a secure electronic signature, nothing in this section shall create any presumption, relating to authenticity and integrity of the electronic record or any electronic signature.
summary: When electronic records or signatures are used in court, the judge will assume they are genuine and haven't been tampered with, unless the other party can prove otherwise. This means that if someone uses a secure electronic record or signature, the court will initially believe it's authentic and hasn't been altered, but this assumption can be challenged if there's evidence to the contrary.

subsections.text: In any proceeding involving a secure electronic record, the Court shall presume unless contrary is proved, that the secure electronic record has not been altered since the specific point of time to which the secure status relates. In any proceeding, involving secure electronic signature, the Court shall presume unless the contrary is proved that

In any proceeding, involving secure electronic signature, the Court shall presume unless the contrary is proved that
the secure electronic signature is affixed by subscriber with the intention of signing or approving the electronic record;

except in the case of a secure electronic record or a secure electronic signature, nothing in this section shall create any presumption, relating to authenticity and integrity of the electronic record or any electronic signature.

title: Presumption as to Electronic Signature Certificates.
content: The Court shall presume, unless contrary is proved, that the information listed in an Electronic Signature Certificate is correct, except for information specified as subscriber information which has not been verified, if the certificate was accepted by the subscriber.
summary: When a court is dealing with an Electronic Signature Certificate, it will assume that the information in the certificate is correct, unless someone proves otherwise. This assumption only applies to the information that has been verified by the subscriber and does not include the unverified subscriber information.

subsections.text: The Court shall presume, unless contrary is proved, that the information listed in an Electronic Signature Certificate is correct, except for information specified as subscriber information which has not been verified, if the certificate was accepted by the subscriber.

title: Presumption as to certified copies of foreign judicial records.
content: (1) The Court may presume that any document purporting to be a certified copy of any judicial record of any country beyond India is genuine and accurate, if the document purports to be certified in any manner which is certified by any representative of the Central Government in or for such country to be the manner commonly in use in that country for the certification of copies of judicial records. 35 (2) An officer who, with respect to any territory or place outside India is a Political Agent therefor, as defined in clause (43) of section 3 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 (10 of 1897), shall, for the purposes of this section, be deemed to be a representative of the Central Government in and for the country comprising that territory or place.
summary: When a court is dealing with a case involving a foreign country, it can assume that a document claiming to be a copy of a foreign court's records is genuine and accurate if it's certified in a way that's commonly accepted in that country. This means if the document looks like it's been officially certified by the foreign country's government, the court will likely accept it as true.

subsections.text: (1) The Court may presume that any document purporting to be a certified copy of any judicial record of any country beyond India is genuine and accurate, if the document purports to be certified in any manner which is certified by any representative of the Central Government in or for such country to be the manner commonly in use in that country for the certification of copies of judicial records. 35 (2) An officer who, with respect to any territory or place outside India is a Political Agent therefor, as defined in clause (43) of section 3 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 (10 of 1897), shall, for the purposes of this section, be deemed to be a representative of the Central Government in and for the country comprising that territory or place.

The Court may presume that any document purporting to be a certified copy of any judicial record of any country beyond India is genuine and accurate, if the document purports to be certified in any manner which is certified by any representative of the Central Government in or for such country to be the manner commonly in use in that country for the certification of copies of judicial records.

An officer who, with respect to any territory or place outside India is a Political Agent therefor, as defined in clause (43) of section 3 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 (10 of 1897), shall, for the purposes of this section, be deemed to be a representative of the Central Government in and for the country comprising that territory or place.

title: Presumption as to books, maps and charts.
content: The Court may presume that any book to which it may refer for information on matters of public or general interest, and that any published map or chart, the statements of which are relevant facts, and which is produced for its inspection, was written and published by the person, and at the time and place, by whom or at which it purports to have been written or published.
summary: In court, if a book, map, or chart is presented as evidence, the judge may assume it's genuine and accurate if it's about a public or general interest topic. This means the judge will consider the book, map, or chart as true unless someone proves it's false. The book, map, or chart must be about a topic that's relevant to the case, and it must be produced in court for everyone to see.

subsections.text: The Court may presume that any book to which it may refer for information on matters of public or general interest, and that any published map or chart, the statements of which are relevant facts, and which is produced for its inspection, was written and published by the person, and at the time and place, by whom or at which it purports to have been written or published.

title: Presumption as to electronic messages.
content: The Court may presume that an electronic message, forwarded by the originator through an electronic mail server to the addressee to whom the message purports to be addressed corresponds with the message as fed into his computer for transmission; but the Court shall not make any presumption as to the person by whom such message was sent.
summary: In court, if someone sends an email or message, the judge can assume that the message sent is the same as what the sender typed into their computer. However, the judge cannot assume who actually sent the message, only that the message itself is genuine.

subsections.text: The Court may presume that an electronic message, forwarded by the originator through an electronic mail server to the addressee to whom the message purports to be addressed corresponds with the message as fed into his computer for transmission; but the Court shall not make any presumption as to the person by whom such message was sent.

title: Presumption as to due execution, etc., of documents not produced.
content: The Court shall presume that every document, called for and not produced after notice to produce, was attested, stamped and executed in the manner required by law.
summary: When a court asks someone to produce a document, but they fail to do so, even after being given a chance, the court will assume that the document was properly attested, stamped, and executed according to the law. This means the court will consider it valid and genuine until proven otherwise.

subsections.text: The Court shall presume that every document, called for and not produced after notice to produce, was attested, stamped and executed in the manner required by law.

title: Presumption as to documents thirty years old.
content: Where any document, purporting or proved to be thirty years old, is produced from any custody which the Court in the particular case considers proper, the Court may presume that the signature and every other part of such document, which purports to be in the handwriting of any particular person, is in that person’s handwriting, and, in the case of a document executed or attested, that it was duly executed and attested by the persons by whom it purports to be executed and attested. Explanation.The Explanation to section 80 shall also apply to this section. Illustrations. (a) A has been in possession of landed property for a long time. He produces from his custody deeds relating to the land showing his titles to it. The custody shall be proper. (b) A produces deeds relating to landed property of which he is the mortgagee. The mortgagor is in possession. The custody shall be proper. (c) A, a connection of B, produces deeds relating to lands in B's possession, which were deposited with him by B for safe custody. The custody shall be proper.
summary:
If a document is at least 30 years old and is produced from a custody that the court considers proper, the court can assume that:

- The signature on the document is genuine and was written by the person it's supposed to be from.
- Any handwritten parts of the document are genuine and were written by the person it's supposed to be from.
- If the document is a signed agreement or was witnessed by someone, the court can assume it was properly signed and witnessed by the people it's supposed to be from.

This rule applies if the person producing the document has been in possession of the property or item related to the document for a long time, or if the document was safely kept by someone else for them.

subsections.text: Where any document, purporting or proved to be thirty years old, is produced from any custody which the Court in the particular case considers proper, the Court may presume that the signature and every other part of such document, which purports to be in the handwriting of any particular person, is in that person’s handwriting, and, in the case of a document executed or attested, that it was duly executed and attested by the persons by whom it purports to be executed and attested.
Explanation.The Explanation to section 80 shall also apply to this section.

Illustrations. (a) A has been in possession of landed property for a long time. He produces from his custody deeds relating to the land showing his titles to it. The custody shall be proper. (b) A produces deeds relating to landed property of which he is the mortgagee. The mortgagor is in possession. The custody shall be proper. (c) A, a connection of B, produces deeds relating to lands in B's possession, which were deposited with him by B for safe custody. The custody shall be proper.

title: Presumption as to electronic records five years old.
content: Where any electronic record, purporting or proved to be five years old, is produced from any custody which the Court in the particular case considers proper, the Court may presume that the electronic signature which purports to be the electronic signature of any particular person was so affixed by him or any person authorised by him in this behalf. Explanation.The Explanation to section 81 shall also apply to this section.
summary: If an electronic record that is at least 5 years old is presented in court and the court believes the record is in a safe custody, it can be assumed that the electronic signature on the record was added by the person who owns it or someone authorized by them.

subsections.text: Where any electronic record, purporting or proved to be five years old, is produced from any custody which the Court in the particular case considers proper, the Court may presume that the electronic signature which purports to be the electronic signature of any particular person was so affixed by him or any person authorised by him in this behalf. Explanation.The Explanation to section 81 shall also apply to this section.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Evidence of terms of contracts, grants and other dispositions of property reduced to form of document.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Exclusion of evidence of oral agreement.
content: When the terms of any such contract, grant or other disposition of property, or any matter required by law to be reduced to the form of a document, have been proved according to section 94, no evidence of any oral agreement or statement shall be admitted, as between the parties to any such instrument or their representatives in interest, for the purpose of contradicting, varying, adding to, or subtracting from, its terms: Provided that any fact may be proved which would invalidate any document, or which would entitle any person to any decree or order relating thereto; such as fraud, intimidation, illegality, want of due execution, want of capacity in any contracting party, want or failure of consideration, or mistake in fact or law: Provided further that the existence of any separate oral agreement as to any matter on which a document is silent, and which is not inconsistent with its terms, may be proved. In considering whether or not this proviso applies, the Court shall have regard to the degree of formality of the document: Provided also that the existence of any separate oral agreement, constituting a condition precedent to the attaching of any obligation under any such contract, grant or disposition of property, may be proved: Provided also that the existence of any distinct subsequent oral agreement to rescind or modify any such contract, grant or disposition of property, may be proved, except in cases in which such contract, grant or disposition of property is by law required to be in writing, or has been registered according to the law in force for the time being as to the registration of documents: Provided also that any usage or custom by which incidents not expressly mentioned in any contract are usually annexed to contracts of that description, may be proved: Provided also that the annexing of such incident would not be repugnant to, or inconsistent with, the express terms of the contract: Provided also that any fact may be proved which shows in what manner the language of a document is related to existing facts. Illustrations. (a) A policy of insurance is effected on goods “in ships from Kolkata to Visakhapatnam”. The goods are shipped in a particular ship which is lost. The fact that particular ship was orally excepted from the policy, cannot be proved. 37 (b) A agrees absolutely in writing to pay B one thousand rupees on the 1st March, 2023. The fact that, at the same time, an oral agreement was made that the money should not be paid till the 31st March, 2023, cannot be proved. (c) An estate called “the Rampur tea estate” is sold by a deed which contains a map of the property sold. The fact that land not included in the map had always been regarded as part of the estate and was meant to pass by the deed cannot be proved. (d) A enters into a written contract with B to work certain mines, the property of B, upon certain terms. A was induced to do so by a misrepresentation of B's as to their value. This fact may be proved. (e) A institutes a suit against B for the specific performance of a contract, and also prays that the contract may be reformed as to one of its provisions, as that provision was inserted in it by mistake. A may prove that such a mistake was made as would by law entitle him to have the contract reformed. (f) A orders goods of B by a letter in which nothing is said as to the time of payment, and accepts the goods on delivery. B sues A for the price. A may show that the goods were supplied on credit for a term still unexpired. (g) A sells B a horse and verbally warrants him sound. A gives B a paper in these words “Bought of A a horse for thirty thousand rupees”. B may prove the verbal warranty. (h) A hires lodgings of B, and gives B a card on which is written “Rooms, ten thousand rupees a month”. A may prove a verbal agreement that these terms were to include partial board. A hires lodging of B for a year, and a regularly stamped agreement, drawn up by an advocate, is made between them. It is silent on the subject of board. A may not prove that board was included in the term verbally. (i) A applies to B for a debt due to A by sending a receipt for the money. B keeps the receipt and does not send the money. In a suit for the amount, A may prove this. (j) A and B make a contract in writing to take effect upon the happening of a certain contingency. The writing is left with B who sues A upon it. A may show the circumstances under which it was delivered.
summary:
When a contract or agreement is in writing, generally, no oral evidence can be used to change, add to, or contradict what's written in the document. This means that if a contract says one thing, you can't use verbal conversations or agreements to say something different. However, there are exceptions to this rule. 

If someone wants to prove that a document is invalid because of fraud, intimidation, or some other problem, they can use oral evidence to show this. For example, if someone was forced to sign a contract under threat, they can use oral evidence to prove this and potentially invalidate the contract.

Additionally, if a document is silent on a particular issue, oral evidence can be used to prove a separate agreement on that issue, as long as it doesn't contradict what's written in the document. For instance, if a rental agreement doesn't mention whether utilities are included, the parties can use oral evidence to prove a separate agreement on this point.

Oral evidence can also be used to prove that a document was supposed to take effect only after certain conditions were met, or that the parties later agreed to cancel or modify the contract. However, if a contract is required by law to be in writing, or if it's been registered, then oral evidence cannot be used to change or cancel it.

Furthermore, oral evidence can be used to prove the customs or practices that are commonly associated with a particular type of contract. For example, in a rental agreement, oral evidence can be used to prove that the landlord normally includes certain services, like maintenance or repairs, even if it's not written in the contract.

Lastly, oral evidence can be used to explain the context and circumstances surrounding a document, such as what the parties meant by certain words or phrases, or how the document relates to real-life events. This can help clarify any ambiguities or uncertainties in the document.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Exclusion of evidence to explain or amend ambiguous document.
content: When the language used in a document is, on its face, ambiguous or defective, evidence may not be given of facts which would show its meaning or supply its defects. Illustrations. (a) A agrees, in writing, to sell a horse to B for “one lakh rupees or one lakh fifty thousand rupees”. Evidence cannot be given to show which price was to be given. (b) A deed contains blanks. Evidence cannot be given of facts which would show how they were meant to be filled.
summary: If a document has unclear or incomplete language, you cannot use evidence to explain what it means or fill in the missing parts. This means that if there's a disagreement about the meaning of a document, the court will interpret it based on what's written, without considering any additional information that might clarify its meaning.

subsections.text: When the language used in a document is, on its face, ambiguous or defective, evidence may not be given of facts which would show its meaning or supply its defects. Illustrations.
A agrees, in writing, to sell a horse to B for “one lakh rupees or one lakh fifty thousand rupees”.
Evidence cannot be given to show which price was to be given.
A deed contains blanks.
Evidence cannot be given of facts which would show how they were meant to be filled.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Exclusion of evidence against application of document to existing facts.
content: When language used in a document is plain in itself, and when it applies accurately to existing facts, evidence may not be given to show that it was not meant to apply to such facts. Illustration. A sells to B, by deed, “my estate at Rampur containing one hundred bighas”. A has an estate at Rampur containing one hundred bighas. Evidence may not be given of the fact that the estate meant to be sold was one situated at a different place and of a different size.
summary: In court, if a document clearly states something and that statement matches the actual facts, you cannot introduce evidence to prove that the document meant something else. This means that if a document says "my estate at Rampur has 100 bighas" and it indeed has 100 bighas, you cannot provide evidence to say that the estate meant to be sold was somewhere else or of a different size. The plain meaning of the document is taken as true in this case.

subsections.text: When language used in a document is plain in itself, and when it applies accurately to existing facts, evidence may not be given to show that it was not meant to apply to such facts. Illustration. A sells to B, by deed, “my estate at Rampur containing one hundred bighas”. A has an estate at Rampur containing one hundred bighas. Evidence may not be given of the fact that the estate meant to be sold was one situated at a different place and of a different size.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Evidence as to document unmeaning in reference to existing facts.
content: When language used in a document is plain in itself, but is unmeaning in reference to existing facts, evidence may be given to show that it was used in a peculiar sense. 38 Illustration. A sells to B, by deed, “my house in Kolkata”. A had no house in Kolkata, but it appears that he had a house at Howrah, of which B had been in possession since the execution of the deed. These facts may be proved to show that the deed related to the house at Howrah.
summary: If a document has clear language, but its meaning is unclear when considering the existing facts, evidence can be presented to show that the language was used in a specific or unusual way. This means that if a document says something that seems straightforward, but the situation suggests it could mean something else, you can provide evidence to explain the intended meaning. For example, if someone sells a house in a document, but it turns out they don't own a house in that location, you can provide evidence to show that the house being referred to is actually in a different location.

subsections.text: When language used in a document is plain in itself, but is unmeaning in reference to existing facts, evidence may be given to show that it was used in a peculiar sense. 38 Illustration. A sells to B, by deed, “my house in Kolkata”. A had no house in Kolkata, but it appears that he had a house at Howrah, of which B had been in possession since the execution of the deed. These facts may be proved to show that the deed related to the house at Howrah.
A sells to B, by deed, “my house in Kolkata”.
A had no house in Kolkata, but it appears that he had a house at Howrah, of which B had been in possession since the execution of the deed.
These facts may be proved to show that the deed related to the house at Howrah.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Evidence as to application of language which can apply to one only of several persons.
content: When the facts are such that the language used might have been meant to apply to any one, and could not have been meant to apply to more than one, of several persons or things, evidence may be given of facts which show which of those persons or things it was intended to apply to.
summary: This section covers cases where a description in a document is clear, but it accidentally fits more than one thing. For example, if you sell 'your white horse' but own two, the court allows you to bring in evidence to prove which specific horse you were talking about. It resolves ambiguity when a description is too general.

subsections.text: When the facts are such that the language used might have been meant to apply to any one, and could not have been meant to apply to more than one, of several persons or things, evidence may be given of facts which show which of those persons or things it was intended to apply to.
A agrees to sell to B, for one thousand rupees, “my white horse”. A has two white horses. Evidence may be given of facts which show which of them was meant.
A agrees to accompany B to Ramgarh. Evidence may be given of facts showing whether Ramgarh in Rajasthan or Ramgarh in Uttarakhand was meant.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Evidence as to application of language to one of two sets of facts, to neither of which the whole correctly applies.
content: When the language used applies partly to one set of existing facts, and partly to another set of existing facts, but the whole of it does not apply correctly to either, evidence may be given to show to which of the two it was meant to apply.
summary: This section deals with 'mixed-up' descriptions. If a document describes land as being 'at Place X and occupied by Person Y,' but you have land at Place X occupied by someone else, and other land occupied by Person Y at a different place, the description is partially wrong for both. The court allows evidence to clarify which property was actually intended for the deal.

subsections.text: When the language used applies partly to one set of existing facts, and partly to another set of existing facts, but the whole of it does not apply correctly to either, evidence may be given to show to which of the two it was meant to apply.

A agrees to sell to B “my land at X in the occupation of Y”. A has land at X, but not in the occupation of Y, and he has land in the occupation of Y but it is not at X. Evidence may be given of facts showing which he meant to sell.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Evidence as to meaning of illegible characters, etc.
content: When the language used applies partly to one set of existing facts, and partly to another set of existing facts, but the whole of it does not apply correctly to either, evidence may be given to show to which of the two it was meant to apply.
summary: This section deals with situations where a legal document or agreement is partially true for two different things, but not fully accurate for either. In such cases, the court allows extra evidence to be presented to clarify which of the two things the person actually intended to refer to. It helps resolve confusion when descriptions are mixed up or overlap.

subsections.text: When the language used applies partly to one set of existing facts, and partly to another set of existing facts, but the whole of it does not apply correctly to either, evidence may be given to show to which of the two it was meant to apply.

A agrees to sell to B “my land at X in the occupation of Y”. A has land at X, but not in the occupation of Y, and he has land in the occupation of Y but it is not at X. Evidence may be given of facts showing which he meant to sell.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Evidence as to meaning of illegible characters, etc.
content: Evidence may be given to show the meaning of illegible or not commonly intelligible characters, of foreign, obsolete, technical, local and regional expressions, of abbreviations and of words used in a peculiar sense. Illustration. A, sculptor, agrees to sell to B, “all my mods”. A has both models and modelling tools. Evidence may be given to show which he meant to sell.
summary:
In court, if there's a word or phrase that's unclear or hard to understand, you can present evidence to explain its meaning. This evidence can help clarify the intended meaning of:

- Words that are written in a way that's hard to read
- Words or phrases from other countries or languages
- Old-fashioned or technical terms
- Local expressions or slang
- Abbreviations
- Words used in a special or unusual way

For example, if a sculptor agrees to sell "all my mods" to someone, and it's unclear whether he means the models or the tools, you can present evidence to show which one he intended to sell.

subsections.text: Evidence may be given to show the meaning of illegible or not commonly intelligible characters, of foreign, obsolete, technical, local and regional expressions, of abbreviations and of words used in a peculiar sense. Illustration. A, sculptor, agrees to sell to B, “all my mods”. A has both models and modelling tools. Evidence may be given to show which he meant to sell.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Who may give evidence of agreement varying terms of document.
content: Persons who are not parties to a document, or their representatives in interest, may give evidence of any facts tending to show a contemporaneous agreement varying the terms of the document. Illustration. A and B make a contract in writing that B shall sell A certain cotton, to be paid for on delivery. At the same time, they make an oral agreement that three months’ credit shall be given to A. This could not be shown as between A and B, but it might be shown by C, if it affected his interests.
summary: In a court of law, if two people, A and B, have made a written agreement, but also made an oral agreement at the same time that changes some terms of the written agreement, then someone who is not a party to this agreement (let's call them C) can provide evidence of this oral agreement if it affects their interests. This means C can tell the court about the oral agreement if it impacts them in some way, even if A and B cannot mention it in court.

chapter: CHAPTER VI — OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
title: Saving of provisions of Indian Succession Act relating to wills.
content: Nothing in this Chapter shall be taken to affect any of the provisions of the Indian Succession Act, 1925 (39 of 1925) as to the construction of wills.
summary: This section of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA) 2023 clarifies that the rules for interpreting wills, as mentioned in the Indian Succession Act of 1925, remain unchanged. It means that the new evidence law does not affect the existing laws related to wills, and the courts will continue to follow the same rules when dealing with wills and their validity.

subsections.text: Nothing in this Chapter shall be taken to affect any of the provisions of the Indian Succession Act, 1925 (39 of 1925) as to the construction of wills.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proof.
content: Whoever desires any Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts must prove that those facts exist, and when a person is bound to prove the existence of any fact, it is said that the burden of proof lies on that person. 39 Illustrations. (a) A desires a Court to give judgment that B shall be punished for a crime which A says B has committed. A must prove that B has committed the crime. (b) A desires a Court to give judgment that he is entitled to certain land in the possession of B, by reason of facts which he asserts, and which B denies, to be true. A must prove the existence of those facts.
summary: In a court of law, if you want to prove that someone has committed a crime or that you have a right to something, you must provide evidence to support your claim. This is called the burden of proof. It means you have to prove that the facts you are claiming are true. If you are the one making a claim, you are responsible for providing the evidence to support it. For example, if you accuse someone of a crime, you must show proof that they actually committed it. If you claim ownership of a piece of land, you must prove that you have the right to it.

subsections.text: Whoever desires any Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts must prove that those facts exist, and when a person is bound to prove the existence of any fact, it is said that the burden of proof lies on that person. 39 Illustrations.
A desires a Court to give judgment that B shall be punished for a crime which A says B has committed.
A must prove that B has committed the crime.
A desires a Court to give judgment that he is entitled to certain land in the possession of B, by reason of facts which he asserts, and which B denies, to be true.
A must prove the existence of those facts.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: On whom burden of proof lies.
content: The burden of proof in a suit or proceeding lies on that person who would fail if no evidence at all were given on either side. Illustrations. (a) A sues B for land of which B is in possession, and which, as A asserts, was left to A by the will of C, B's father. If no evidence were given on either side, B would be entitled to retain his possession. Therefore, the burden of proof is on A. (b) A sues B for money due on a bond. The execution of the bond is admitted, but B says that it was obtained by fraud, which A denies. If no evidence were given on either side, A would succeed, as the bond is not disputed and the fraud is not proved. Therefore, the burden of proof is on B.
summary:
In a court case, the person who needs to prove their claim is the one who will lose if there is no evidence presented from either side. This means if you're suing someone, you need to provide evidence to support your claim, and if you don't, the other person gets to keep what they have. 

For example, if you're claiming a piece of land was left to you by someone's will, but the other person is already in possession of it, you need to prove your claim to get the land. If you can't prove it, the other person gets to keep the land. 

Similarly, if you're claiming someone owes you money from a bond, but the other person says it was obtained by fraud, you need to prove that the bond was not obtained by fraud. If you can't prove it, the other person gets to keep the money.

subsections.text: The burden of proof in a suit or proceeding lies on that person who would fail if no evidence at all were given on either side. Illustrations.
A sues B for land of which B is in possession, and which, as A asserts, was left to A by the will of C, B's father.
If no evidence were given on either side, B would be entitled to retain his possession. Therefore, the burden of proof is on A.
A sues B for money due on a bond.
The execution of the bond is admitted, but B says that it was obtained by fraud, which A denies.
If no evidence were given on either side, A would succeed, as the bond is not disputed and the fraud is not proved. Therefore, the burden of proof is on B.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proof as to particular fact.
content: The burden of proof as to any particular fact lies on that person who wishes the Court to believe in its existence, unless it is provided by any law that the proof of that fact shall lie on any particular person. Illustration. A prosecutes B for theft, and wishes the Court to believe that B admitted the theft to C. A must prove the admission. B wishes the Court to believe that, at the time in question, he was elsewhere. He must prove it.
summary: In a court of law, the person who wants to prove a specific fact must provide evidence to support it. This is called the 'burden of proof'. If someone accuses another person of doing something, like stealing, they must provide evidence to prove that accusation. On the other hand, if the accused person wants to prove their innocence, they must also provide evidence to support their claim. In other words, each side must prove their own case, and the burden of proof lies with the person who is making the claim.

subsections.text: The burden of proof as to any particular fact lies on that person who wishes the Court to believe in its existence, unless it is provided by any law that the proof of that fact shall lie on any particular person. Illustration. A prosecutes B for theft, and wishes the Court to believe that B admitted the theft to C. A must prove the admission. B wishes the Court to believe that, at the time in question, he was elsewhere. He must prove it.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proving fact to be proved to make evidence admissible.
content: The burden of proving any fact necessary to be proved in order to enable any person to give evidence of any other fact is on the person who wishes to give such evidence. Illustrations. (a) A wishes to prove a dying declaration by B. A must prove B's death. (b) A wishes to prove, by secondary evidence, the contents of a lost document. A must prove that the document has been lost.
summary: When someone wants to use evidence in court, they need to prove that the evidence is valid or relevant to the case. This means they have to show that the evidence is genuine and not fake. For example, if someone wants to use a dying declaration as evidence, they need to prove that the person who made the declaration is actually dead. Similarly, if someone wants to use a copy of a lost document as evidence, they need to prove that the original document is indeed lost. This is called the "burden of proof" and it's the responsibility of the person who wants to use the evidence to show that it's valid.

subsections.text: The burden of proving any fact necessary to be proved in order to enable any person to give evidence of any other fact is on the person who wishes to give such evidence. Illustrations. (a) A wishes to prove a dying declaration by B. A must prove B's death. (b) A wishes to prove, by secondary evidence, the contents of a lost document. A must prove that the document has been lost.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proving that case of accused comes within exceptions.
content: When a person is accused of any offence, the burden of proving the existence of circumstances bringing the case within any of the General Exceptions in the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or within any special exception or proviso contained in any other part of the said Sanhita, or in any law defining the offence, is upon him, and the Court shall presume the absence of such circumstances. Illustrations. (a) A, accused of murder, alleges that, by reason of unsoundness of mind, he did not know the nature of the act. The burden of proof is on A. (b) A, accused of murder, alleges that, by grave and sudden provocation, he was deprived of the power of self-control. The burden of proof is on A. (c) Section 117 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 provides that whoever, except in the case provided for by sub-section (2) of section 122, voluntarily causes grievous hurt, shall be subject to certain punishments. A is charged with voluntarily causing grievous hurt under section 117. The burden of proving the circumstances bringing the case under sub-section (2) of section 122 lies on A. 40
summary:
When someone is accused of a crime, they must prove that their actions fall under specific exceptions in the law that might reduce their punishment or make them not guilty. These exceptions include things like being insane, having a good reason for their actions, or meeting certain conditions that make their actions less serious. 

For example, if someone is accused of murder and claims they were insane, they must prove that they were indeed insane at the time of the crime. If they fail to do so, the court will assume they were sane. Similarly, if someone is accused of causing harm and claims they did it due to a sudden provocation, they must prove that they were indeed provoked and couldn't control themselves. 

In general, the person accused of a crime has the burden of proving that their actions fit within these exceptions, and if they fail to do so, the court will assume the exceptions do not apply.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proving fact especially within knowledge.
content: When any fact is especially within the knowledge of any person, the burden of proving that fact is upon him. Illustrations. (a) When a person does an act with some intention other than that which the character and circumstances of the act suggest, the burden of proving that intention is upon him. (b) A is charged with travelling on a railway without a ticket. The burden of proving that he had a ticket is on him.
summary: If a person knows something that can prove a fact in a case, they are responsible for proving that fact. This means if a person's actions or circumstances suggest one thing, but they claim it's something else, they need to provide evidence to prove their claim. For example, if someone is accused of traveling on a train without a ticket, they must prove they had a ticket to avoid being found guilty.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proving death of person known to have been alive within thirty years.
content: When the question is whether a man is alive or dead, and it is shown that he was alive within thirty years, the burden of proving that he is dead is on the person who affirms it.
summary: In India, if someone claims that a person is dead, but there's proof that the person was alive within the last 30 years, the person making the claim (that the person is dead) must provide evidence to prove it. They have the burden of proof to show that the person has indeed passed away.

subsections.text: When the question is whether a man is alive or dead, and it is shown that he was alive within thirty years, the burden of proving that he is dead is on the person who affirms it.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proving that person is alive who has not been heard of for seven years.
content: When the question is whether a man is alive or dead, and it is proved that he has not been heard of for seven years by those who would naturally have heard of him if he had been alive, the burden of proving that he is alive is shifted to the person who affirms it.
summary: This is the legal rule for missing persons. If someone has not been heard from for 7 years by their family or friends who would normally be in touch, the law presumes they are dead. If someone else wants to claim that the missing person is still alive (for example, to stop an inheritance), they must prove it with evidence.

subsections.text: When the question is whether a man is alive or dead, and it is proved that he has not been heard of for seven years by those who would naturally have heard of him if he had been alive, the burden of proving that he is alive is shifted to the person who affirms it.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proof as to relationship in the cases of partners, landlord and tenant, principal and agent.
content: When the question is whether persons are partners, landlord and tenant, or principal and agent, and it has been shown that they have been acting as such, the burden of proving that they do not stand, or have ceased to stand, to each other in those relationships respectively, is on the person who affirms it.
summary: This section deals with existing legal relationships. If two people have been acting as partners, or as a landlord and tenant, the law assumes that relationship exists. If one of them suddenly claims they are no longer in that relationship (e.g., claiming a lease has ended or a partnership is dissolved), the burden is on them to prove that the relationship has stopped.

subsections.text: When the question is whether persons are partners, landlord and tenant, or principal and agent, and it has been shown that they have been acting as such, the burden of proving that they do not stand, or have ceased to stand, to each other in those relationships respectively, is on the person who affirms it.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Burden of proof as to ownership.
content: When the question is whether any person is owner of anything of which he is shown to be in possession, the burden of proving that he is not the owner is on the person who affirms that he is not the owner.
summary: When a person is in possession of something, like a house or a car, and someone questions whether they actually own it, the person who claims they don't own it must prove it in court. The person who is in possession of the item does not have to prove they own it; instead, the person who says they don't own it must provide evidence to support their claim.

subsections.text: When the question is whether any person is owner of anything of which he is shown to be in possession, the burden of proving that he is not the owner is on the person who affirms that he is not the owner.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Proof of good faith in transactions where one party is in relation of active confidence.
content: Where there is a question as to the good faith of a transaction between parties, one of whom stands to the other in a position of active confidence, the burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the party who is in a position of active confidence. Illustrations. (a) The good faith of a sale by a client to an advocate is in question in a suit brought by the client. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the advocate. (b) The good faith of a sale by a son just come of age to a father is in question in a suit brought by the son. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the father.
summary: When two people are in a situation where one person has more power or trust over the other, such as a client and their lawyer, or a child and their parent, the person with more power must prove that any transaction they made with the other person was done in good faith. This means they must show that they were honest and not trying to take advantage of the other person. If the person with more power cannot prove this, the court may assume that the transaction was not done in good faith.

subsections.text: Where there is a question as to the good faith of a transaction between parties, one of whom stands to the other in a position of active confidence, the burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the party who is in a position of active confidence. Illustrations.
The good faith of a sale by a client to an advocate is in question in a suit brought by the client. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the advocate.
The good faith of a sale by a son just come of age to a father is in question in a suit brought by the son. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the father.

chapter: CHAPTER VII — OF THE BURDEN OF PROOF
title: Presumption as to certain offences.
content: (1) Where a person is accused of having committed any offence specified in sub-section (2), in (a) any area declared to be a disturbed area under any enactment for the time being in force, making provision for the suppression of disorder and restoration and maintenance of public order; or (b) any area in which there has been, over a period of more than one month, extensive disturbance of the public peace, and it is shown that such person had been at a place in such area at a time when firearms or explosives were used at or from that place to attack or resist the members of any armed forces or the forces charged with the maintenance of public order acting in the discharge of their duties, it shall be presumed, unless the contrary is shown, that such person had committed such offence. (2) The offences referred to in sub-section (1) are the following, namely: (a) an offence under section 147, section 148, section 149 or section 150 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; (b) criminal conspiracy or attempt to commit, or abetment of, an offence under section 149 or section 150 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. 41
summary:
This section of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 2023, explains a special rule for proving certain crimes in court. 

If someone is accused of committing a crime in a specific area where there has been violence or public unrest, and it is shown that they were present at the scene when firearms or explosives were used, the court will assume they are guilty of the crime unless the accused can prove otherwise. 

This rule applies to specific crimes, such as rioting, causing harm to others, or conspiring to commit these crimes. The court will make this assumption to help prove the case, but the accused can still try to show that they are not guilty.

(2) The offences referred to in sub-section (1) are the following, namely: (a) an offence under section 147, section 148, section 149 or section 150 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; (b) criminal conspiracy or attempt to commit, or abetment of, an offence under section 149 or section 150 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
